data.title: Crucial Detail: Sign Defaults
data.text: Always remember to include the correct sign (+ for endothermic, - for exothermic) in your final enthalpy value. A naked number implies positive but examiners frequently deduct marks if the explicit + is missing.

data.title: Standard Conditions Definition
data.text: Make sure you explicitly mention 100 kPa (not 1 atm) and the specified temperature (always state 298 K) when defining standard test conditions.

data.title: Activation Energy Arrow Direction
data.text: In reaction profiles, your activation energy (Ea) arrow must point upwards starting exactly from the reactant line to the peak of the curve. Bidirectional or downward-pointing arrows will lose the mark.

data.title: Zero Enthalpy of Formation
data.text: Remember that the standard enthalpy of formation for an element exactly in its standard stable state (e.g. O₂(g), C(graphite)) is absolutely zero.

data.title: Calorimetry Error: Mass Parameter
data.text: When using q = mcΔT, 'm' is the mass of the solution changing temperature (e.g., volume of water + acid + alkaline in neutralisations), NEVER just the mass of the solid dissolved.

data.title: Hess's Law Cycle Reversals
data.text: When following a Hess's Law cycle, if you physically trace against the direction of an arrow (reversing the reaction), you absolutely must flip the sign of that enthalpy value.

data.title: Mean Bond Enthalpies Formula
data.text: Examiners prefer using: ΔH = Sum of bonds broken (endothermic +) MINUS Sum of bonds formed (exothermic -). Just be very careful with negative signs if you calculate bonds formed as negative values beforehand.

data.title: Bond Enthalpy vs Polarity
data.text: For halogenoalkane hydrolysis rate questions, explicitly mention that bond enthalpy (C-Hal bond strength) is the dominant factor controlling the rate of reaction, overcoming the opposite trend in bond polarity.

data.title: Thermodynamic vs Kinetic Stability
data.text: In kinetics, thoroughly distinguish between thermodynamic stability (enthalpy/ΔH rules) and kinetic stability (activation energy/Ea holds up the reaction).

data.title: London Forces Explanation
data.text: London forces exist in all molecular substances. Their strength fundamentally increases as the number of electrons in the molecule increases (do not just say 'greater mass').

data.title: Critical Elements of Hydrogen Bonding
data.text: When drawing hydrogen bonds, always show three things: (1) the lone pair of electrons acting as the acceptor, (2) the partial charges (δ+ and δ-) on BOTH molecules, and (3) the dashed/dotted line for the H-bond itself.

data.title: Ice Density Anomaly
data.text: Explain ice's structurally lower density by explicitly describing the open lattice structure fixed by maximum hydrogen bonding holding molecules geometrically further apart than in liquid water.

data.title: Branching and Boiling Point
data.text: Branching in isomers drastically decreases the surface area of contact between molecules, permanently weakening London forces and lowering the boiling point. Ensure you use the term 'surface area of contact'.

data.title: Solubility Energy Analysis
data.text: When explaining solubility theoretically, explicitly state that energy released by establishing new solute-solvent interactions must be comparable or greater to the energy needed to break old solute-solute and solvent-solvent ones.

data.title: Beyond 'Like Dissolves Like'
data.text: Remember the heuristic 'like dissolves like', but examiners require you to explicitly back it up by analytically comparing the specific intermolecular forces present before and after mixing.

data.title: Ionic Hydration Accuracy
data.text: In ionic hydration diagrams, water molecules surround the ions meticulously: their δ- oxygen MUST be directed perfectly towards cations, and δ+ hydrogens towards anions.

data.title: Bonds vs Intermolecular Forces
data.text: Be precise: don't just generically say 'bonds are broken'—always explicitly specify whether you mean strong intramolecular covalent bonds or weak intermolecular forces (e.g. London dispersion).

data.title: Comparing Magnitudes Properly
data.text: Ensure you explicitly specify the differing type of intermolecular forces dominating each substance, not just the magnitude, when answering comparative long-answer properties questions.
